[[July 1]]–[[July 15]] (Hakata Gion Yamakasa) | map = Fukuoka city }} {{Nihongo|'''Kushida-jinja'''|櫛田神社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]], [[Japan]]. Dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo]], it is said to have been founded in 757. The [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] [[Matsuri|festival]] is centred on the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/showcase/english/theme/db_object_37.html |title=Kushida Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=24 March 2011}} ==Overview== '''Kushida Shrine''' (くしだじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Kamikawabatamachi, [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. It hosts festivals such as the [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] in July and the [[Hakata Okunchi]] in October. Although the [[Hakata Matsubayashi]] (Hakata Dontaku) in May is not strictly a Kushida Shrine festival, the Matsubayashi procession traditionally starts from Kushida Shrine{{Cite book|author=Kiyoshi Tanaka, Osamu Matsuda, Noriko Obayashi, Takashi Tomokiyo |title=Hakata Tabijō: Shiki no Gyoji Rekishi Yomoyama |publisher=Eizo Yagi |date=1967-04-25 |page=88}}{{Cite book|author=Seizo Inoue |title=Hakata Kyōdo Shi Jiten |publisher=Sanda Hisamoto |date=1987-11-01 |pages=264-266}}. == festival Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities: [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami), [[Amaterasu Ōmikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami). The main hall enshrines Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the left hall enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami, and the right hall enshrines Susanoo no Ōkami. Unlike other Kushida Shrines across Japan that mainly enshrine [[Kushinadahime]], Kushinadahime is not enshrined here, although there is a theory that this shrine originally enshrined her. Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami (Kushida no Ōkami) is believed to be a deity transferred from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]], [[Ise Province]]. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. There is a shrine dedicated to Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami in [[Sado City]], and among the five shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime, three are located in the Hokuriku region. == History == According to shrine tradition, Kushida Shrine was established in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) when the deity from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]] was transferred here, along with Amaterasu Ōmikami, who served the clan of Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the deity of the Matsusaka shrine. In [[Tengyō]] 4 (941), Ono no Yoshifuru prayed at [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto to suppress the [[Rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] and subsequently transferred Susanoo no Ōkami to this shrine after pacifying the rebellion. However, the most widely accepted theory is that in the late Heian period, [[Taira no Kiyomori]] transferred [[Kushida Shrine (Saga)|Kushida Shrine]] in [[Kanzaki]], [[Hizen Province]] (now Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture) to Hakata, a base for Japan-Song trade. This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kushida-jinja

[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami) | Shrine rank = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]], [[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) | festival = [[May 3]]–[[May 4]] (Hakata Dontaku)
[[July 1]]–[[July 15]] (Hakata Gion Yamakasa) | map = Fukuoka city }} {{Nihongo|'''Kushida-jinja'''|櫛田神社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]], [[Japan]]. Dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo]], it is said to have been founded in 757. The [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] [[Matsuri|festival]] is centred on the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/showcase/english/theme/db_object_37.html |title=Kushida Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=24 March 2011}} ==Overview== '''Kushida Shrine''' (くしだじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Kamikawabatamachi, [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. It hosts festivals such as the [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] in July and the [[Hakata Okunchi]] in October. Although the [[Hakata Matsubayashi]] (Hakata Dontaku) in May is not strictly a Kushida Shrine festival, the Matsubayashi procession traditionally starts from Kushida Shrine{{Cite book|author=Kiyoshi Tanaka, Osamu Matsuda, Noriko Obayashi, Takashi Tomokiyo |title=Hakata Tabijō: Shiki no Gyoji Rekishi Yomoyama |publisher=Eizo Yagi |date=1967-04-25 |page=88}}{{Cite book|author=Seizo Inoue |title=Hakata Kyōdo Shi Jiten |publisher=Sanda Hisamoto |date=1987-11-01 |pages=264-266}}. == festival Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities: [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami), [[Amaterasu Ōmikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami). The main hall enshrines Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the left hall enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami, and the right hall enshrines Susanoo no Ōkami. Unlike other Kushida Shrines across Japan that mainly enshrine [[Kushinadahime]], Kushinadahime is not enshrined here, although there is a theory that this shrine originally enshrined her. Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami (Kushida no Ōkami) is believed to be a deity transferred from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]], [[Ise Province]]. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. There is a shrine dedicated to Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami in [[Sado City]], and among the five shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime, three are located in the Hokuriku region. == History == According to shrine tradition, Kushida Shrine was established in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) when the deity from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]] was transferred here, along with Amaterasu Ōmikami, who served the clan of Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the deity of the Matsusaka shrine. In [[Tengyō]] 4 (941), Ono no Yoshifuru prayed at [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto to suppress the [[Rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] and subsequently transferred Susanoo no Ōkami to this shrine after pacifying the rebellion. However, the most widely accepted theory is that in the late Heian period, [[Taira no Kiyomori]] transferred [[Kushida Shrine (Saga)|Kushida Shrine]] in [[Kanzaki]], [[Hizen Province]] (now Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture) to Hakata, a base for Japan-Song trade. This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. Karatsu City. Archived from the original on April 19, 2021. associated with [[Hata no Chikahira|Hata no Mikawa no Kami Chikahira]] of the [[Matsuura Clan]], whose peonies decorate the Yamakasa, was transplanted from Hizen Town, Karatsu City, in [[2018]] (Heisei 30){{Cite web|title=Hakata Gion Yamakasa and Peony Connection - Kushida Shrine in Fukuoka Receives Peony from Karatsu|url=https://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/308161|website=Saga Newspaper LiVE|accessdate=2019-12-02|language=ja}}. In [[1955]] (Showa 30), Typhoon No. 22 caused damage such as toppling an [[Enoki]] tree in the precincts{{Cite web|title=Kushida Shrine - Fallen Enoki Tree - Nishinippon Photo Library|url=http://c.nishinippon.co.jp/photolibrary/cat672/post_1691.php|website=c.nishinippon.co.jp|accessdate=2020-04-08|language=ja}}. == Gallery == File:Fukuoka Kushida kazariyama.jpg|Decorated Yamakasa float in the precincts File:Fukuoka kushida ginnan.jpg|The 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree, Kushida no Ginnan File:20100720 Fukuoka Kushida 3614.jpg|Celestial direction chart on the ceiling of the tower gate File:Fukuoka Kushida Setsubun 2011.jpg|Giant Otafuku mask displayed around Setsubun File:Kushida Shrine02.jpg|Ema (votive plaques) in the precincts File:Kushida Shrine04.jpg|Chōzuya [[July 1]]–[[July 15]] (Hakata Gion Yamakasa) | map = Fukuoka city }} {{Nihongo|'''Kushida-jinja'''|櫛田神社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]], [[Japan]]. Dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo]], it is said to have been founded in 757. The [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] [[Matsuri|festival]] is centred on the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/showcase/english/theme/db_object_37.html |title=Kushida Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=24 March 2011}} ==Overview== '''Kushida Shrine''' (くしだじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Kamikawabatamachi, [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. It hosts festivals such as the [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] in July and the [[Hakata Okunchi]] in October. Although the [[Hakata Matsubayashi]] (Hakata Dontaku) in May is not strictly a Kushida Shrine festival, the Matsubayashi procession traditionally starts from Kushida Shrine{{Cite book|author=Kiyoshi Tanaka, Osamu Matsuda, Noriko Obayashi, Takashi Tomokiyo |title=Hakata Tabijō: Shiki no Gyoji Rekishi Yomoyama |publisher=Eizo Yagi |date=1967-04-25 |page=88}}{{Cite book|author=Seizo Inoue |title=Hakata Kyōdo Shi Jiten |publisher=Sanda Hisamoto |date=1987-11-01 |pages=264-266}}. == festival Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities: [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami), [[Amaterasu Ōmikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami). The main hall enshrines Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the left hall enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami, and the right hall enshrines Susanoo no Ōkami. Unlike other Kushida Shrines across Japan that mainly enshrine [[Kushinadahime]], Kushinadahime is not enshrined here, although there is a theory that this shrine originally enshrined her. Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami (Kushida no Ōkami) is believed to be a deity transferred from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]], [[Ise Province]]. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. There is a shrine dedicated to Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami in [[Sado City]], and among the five shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime, three are located in the Hokuriku region. == History == According to shrine tradition, Kushida Shrine was established in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) when the deity from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]] was transferred here, along with Amaterasu Ōmikami, who served the clan of Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the deity of the Matsusaka shrine. In [[Tengyō]] 4 (941), Ono no Yoshifuru prayed at [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto to suppress the [[Rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] and subsequently transferred Susanoo no Ōkami to this shrine after pacifying the rebellion. However, the most widely accepted theory is that in the late Heian period, [[Taira no Kiyomori]] transferred [[Kushida Shrine (Saga)|Kushida Shrine]] in [[Kanzaki]], [[Hizen Province]] (now Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture) to Hakata, a base for Japan-Song trade. This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. Karatsu City. Archived from the original on April 19, 2021. associated with [[Hata no Chikahira|Hata no Mikawa no Kami Chikahira]] of the [[Matsuura Clan]], whose peonies decorate the Yamakasa, was transplanted from Hizen Town, Karatsu City, in [[2018]] (Heisei 30){{Cite web|title=Hakata Gion Yamakasa and Peony Connection - Kushida Shrine in Fukuoka Receives Peony from Karatsu|url=https://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/308161|website=Saga Newspaper LiVE|accessdate=2019-12-02|language=ja}}. In [[1955]] (Showa 30), Typhoon No. 22 caused damage such as toppling an [[Enoki]] tree in the precincts{{Cite web|title=Kushida Shrine - Fallen Enoki Tree - Nishinippon Photo Library|url=http://c.nishinippon.co.jp/photolibrary/cat672/post_1691.php|website=c.nishinippon.co.jp|accessdate=2020-04-08|language=ja}}. == Gallery == File:Fukuoka Kushida kazariyama.jpg|Decorated [[July 1]]–[[July 15]] (Hakata Gion Yamakasa) | map = Fukuoka city }} {{Nihongo|'''Kushida-jinja'''|櫛田神社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]], [[Japan]]. Dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo]], it is said to have been founded in 757. The [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] [[Matsuri|festival]] is centred on the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/showcase/english/theme/db_object_37.html |title=Kushida Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=24 March 2011}} ==Overview== '''Kushida Shrine''' (くしだじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Kamikawabatamachi, [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. It hosts festivals such as the [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] in July and the [[Hakata Okunchi]] in October. Although the [[Hakata Matsubayashi]] (Hakata Dontaku) in May is not strictly a Kushida Shrine festival, the Matsubayashi procession traditionally starts from Kushida Shrine{{Cite book|author=Kiyoshi Tanaka, Osamu Matsuda, Noriko Obayashi, Takashi Tomokiyo |title=Hakata Tabijō: Shiki no Gyoji Rekishi Yomoyama |publisher=Eizo Yagi |date=1967-04-25 |page=88}}{{Cite book|author=Seizo Inoue |title=Hakata Kyōdo Shi Jiten |publisher=Sanda Hisamoto |date=1987-11-01 |pages=264-266}}. == festival Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities: [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami), [[Amaterasu Ōmikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami). The main hall enshrines Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the left hall enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami, and the right hall enshrines Susanoo no Ōkami. Unlike other Kushida Shrines across Japan that mainly enshrine [[Kushinadahime]], Kushinadahime is not enshrined here, although there is a theory that this shrine originally enshrined her. Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami (Kushida no Ōkami) is believed to be a deity transferred from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]], [[Ise Province]]. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. There is a shrine dedicated to Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami in [[Sado City]], and among the five shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime, three are located in the Hokuriku region. == History == According to shrine tradition, Kushida Shrine was established in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) when the deity from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]] was transferred here, along with Amaterasu Ōmikami, who served the clan of Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the deity of the Matsusaka shrine. In [[Tengyō]] 4 (941), Ono no Yoshifuru prayed at [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto to suppress the [[Rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] and subsequently transferred Susanoo no Ōkami to this shrine after pacifying the rebellion. However, the most widely accepted theory is that in the late Heian period, [[Taira no Kiyomori]] transferred [[Kushida Shrine (Saga)|Kushida Shrine]] in [[Kanzaki]], [[Hizen Province]] (now Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture) to Hakata, a base for Japan-Song trade. This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. Karatsu City. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kushida-jinja
櫛田神社 | image = Kushidajinjafukuoka01.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|34.7|N|130|24|37.7|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo]] | founded_by = | established = 757 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-41, Kamikawabata-machi, [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]]
[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] 812-0026 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kushida Shrine | image = [[File:Kushidajinjafukuoka01.jpg|250px]]
main hall | location = 1-41 Kamikawabatamachi, Hakata-ku, [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] | ISO = JP-40 | type degrees = 33 | type minutes = 35 | type seconds = 34.641 | longitude degrees = 130 | longitude minutes = 24 | longitude seconds = 37.814 | Label Position = left | festival Deities = [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami|Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami)
[[Amaterasu Ōmikami]]
[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami) | Shrine rank = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]], [[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) | festival = [[May 3]]–[[May 4]] (Hakata Dontaku)
[[July 1]]–[[July 15]] (Hakata Gion Yamakasa) | map = Fukuoka city }} {{Nihongo|'''Kushida-jinja'''|櫛田神社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]], [[Japan]]. Dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo]], it is said to have been founded in 757. The [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] [[Matsuri|festival]] is centred on the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/showcase/english/theme/db_object_37.html |title=Kushida Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=24 March 2011}} ==Overview== '''Kushida Shrine''' (くしだじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Kamikawabatamachi, [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. It hosts festivals such as the [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] in July and the [[Hakata Okunchi]] in October. Although the [[Hakata Matsubayashi]] (Hakata Dontaku) in May is not strictly a Kushida Shrine festival, the Matsubayashi procession traditionally starts from Kushida Shrine{{Cite book|author=Kiyoshi Tanaka, Osamu Matsuda, Noriko Obayashi, Takashi Tomokiyo |title=Hakata Tabijō: Shiki no Gyoji Rekishi Yomoyama |publisher=Eizo Yagi |date=1967-04-25 |page=88}}{{Cite book|author=Seizo Inoue |title=Hakata Kyōdo Shi Jiten |publisher=Sanda Hisamoto |date=1987-11-01 |pages=264-266}}. == festival Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities: [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami), [[Amaterasu Ōmikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami). The main hall enshrines Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the left hall enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami, and the right hall enshrines Susanoo no Ōkami. Unlike other Kushida Shrines across Japan that mainly enshrine [[Kushinadahime]], Kushinadahime is not enshrined here, although there is a theory that this shrine originally enshrined her. Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami (Kushida no Ōkami) is believed to be a deity transferred from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]], [[Ise Province]]. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. There is a shrine dedicated to Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami in [[Sado City]], and among the five shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime, three are located in the Hokuriku region. == History == According to shrine tradition, Kushida Shrine was established in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) when the deity from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]] was transferred here, along with Amaterasu Ōmikami, who served the clan of Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the deity of the Matsusaka shrine. In [[Tengyō]] 4 (941), Ono no Yoshifuru prayed at [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto to suppress the [[Rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] and subsequently transferred Susanoo no Ōkami to this shrine after pacifying the rebellion. However, the most widely accepted theory is that in the late Heian period, [[Taira no Kiyomori]] transferred [[Kushida Shrine (Saga)|Kushida Shrine]] in [[Kanzaki]], [[Hizen Province]] (now Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture) to Hakata, a base for Japan-Song trade. This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. Karatsu City. Archived from the original on April 19, 2021. associated with [[Hata no Chikahira|Hata no Mikawa no Kami Chikahira]] of the [[Matsuura Clan]], whose peonies decorate the Yamakasa, was transplanted from Hizen Town, Karatsu City, in [[2018]] (Heisei 30){{Cite web|title=Hakata Gion Yamakasa and Peony Connection - Kushida Shrine in Fukuoka Receives Peony from Karatsu|url=https://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/308161|website=Saga Newspaper LiVE|accessdate=2019-12-02|language=ja}}. In [[1955]] (Showa 30), Typhoon No. 22 caused damage such as toppling an [[Enoki]] tree in the precincts{{Cite web|title=Kushida Shrine - Fallen Enoki Tree - Nishinippon Photo Library|url=http://c.nishinippon.co.jp/photolibrary/cat672/post_1691.php|website=c.nishinippon.co.jp|accessdate=2020-04-08|language=ja}}. == Gallery == File:Fukuoka Kushida kazariyama.jpg|Decorated Yamakasa float in the precincts File:Fukuoka kushida ginnan.jpg|The 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree, Kushida no Ginnan File:20100720 Fukuoka Kushida 3614.jpg|Celestial direction chart on the ceiling of the tower gate File:Fukuoka Kushida Setsubun 2011.jpg|Giant Otafuku mask displayed around Setsubun File:Kushida Shrine02.jpg|Ema (votive plaques) in the precincts File:Kushida Shrine04.jpg|Chōzuya (purification pavilion) in the precincts File:Kushida shrine in Hakata wharf.jpg|Hama-miya at Hakata Wharf == Transportation == * [[Fukuoka City Subway]] [[Fukuoka City Subway Nanakuma Line|Nanakuma Line]] [[Kushida Shrine Station]] - 1 minute walk (140m). * [[Fukuoka City Subway]] [[Fukuoka City Subway Kūkō Line|Kūkō Line]] [[Gion Station (Fukuoka)|Gion Station]] - 5 minutes walk (400m). * Fukuoka City Subway Kūkō Line and [[Fukuoka City Subway Hakozaki Line|Hakozaki Line]] [[Nakasu-Kawabata Station]] - 8 minutes walk (660m). * [[Nishitetsu Bus]] - Get off at "Canal City Hakata-mae" or "Canal East Building-mae" bus stop. * [[Fukuoka Expressway Ring Road]] [[Gofukumachi Interchange]] - 1.2km. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{Citation style|date=March 2018|section=1}} * Sukenari Azusa, Yoshihiko Umeda, editors and supervisors, ''Shinto Dictionary,'' Jinja Shinpōsha, [[1968]], p. 26. * Eiji Shirai, Masanori Toki, editors, ''Shrine Dictionary,'' [[Tōkyōdō Shuppan]], [[1979]], pp. 123-124. == External Links == {{commonscat|Kushida-jinja, Fukuoka}} * [https://www.hakatayamakasa.com/61866.html Kushida Shrine] - Hakata Gion Yamakasa official [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami) | Shrine rank = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]], [[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) | festival = [[May 3]]–[[May 4]] (Hakata Dontaku)
[[July 1]]–[[July 15]] (Hakata Gion Yamakasa) | map = Fukuoka city }} {{Nihongo|'''Kushida-jinja'''|櫛田神社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]], [[Japan]]. Dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo]], it is said to have been founded in 757. The [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] [[Matsuri|festival]] is centred on the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/showcase/english/theme/db_object_37.html |title=Kushida Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=24 March 2011}} ==Overview== '''Kushida Shrine''' (くしだじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Kamikawabatamachi, [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. It hosts festivals such as the [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] in July and the [[Hakata Okunchi]] in October. Although the [[Hakata Matsubayashi]] (Hakata Dontaku) in May is not strictly a Kushida Shrine festival, the Matsubayashi procession traditionally starts from Kushida Shrine{{Cite book|author=Kiyoshi Tanaka, Osamu Matsuda, Noriko Obayashi, Takashi Tomokiyo |title=Hakata Tabijō: Shiki no Gyoji Rekishi Yomoyama |publisher=Eizo Yagi |date=1967-04-25 |page=88}}{{Cite book|author=Seizo Inoue |title=Hakata Kyōdo Shi Jiten |publisher=Sanda Hisamoto |date=1987-11-01 |pages=264-266}}. == festival Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities: [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami), [[Amaterasu Ōmikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami). The main hall enshrines Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the left hall enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami, and the right hall enshrines Susanoo no Ōkami. Unlike other Kushida Shrines across Japan that mainly enshrine [[Kushinadahime]], Kushinadahime is not enshrined here, although there is a theory that this shrine originally enshrined her. Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami (Kushida no Ōkami) is believed to be a deity transferred from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]], [[Ise Province]]. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. There is a shrine dedicated to Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami in [[Sado City]], and among the five shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime, three are located in the Hokuriku region. == History == According to shrine tradition, Kushida Shrine was established in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) when the deity from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]] was transferred here, along with Amaterasu Ōmikami, who served the clan of Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the deity of the Matsusaka shrine. In [[Tengyō]] 4 (941), Ono no Yoshifuru prayed at [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto to suppress the [[Rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] and subsequently transferred Susanoo no Ōkami to this shrine after pacifying the rebellion. However, the most widely accepted theory is that in the late Heian period, [[Taira no Kiyomori]] transferred [[Kushida Shrine (Saga)|Kushida Shrine]] in [[Kanzaki]], [[Hizen Province]] (now Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture) to Hakata, a base for Japan-Song trade. This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. Karatsu City. Archived from the original on April 19, 2021. associated with [[Hata no Chikahira|Hata no Mikawa no Kami Chikahira]] of the [[Matsuura Clan]], whose peonies decorate the Yamakasa, was transplanted from Hizen Town, Karatsu City, in [[2018]] (Heisei 30){{Cite web|title=Hakata Gion Yamakasa and Peony Connection - Kushida Shrine in Fukuoka Receives Peony from Karatsu|url=https://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/308161|website=Saga Newspaper LiVE|accessdate=2019-12-02|language=ja}}. In [[1955]] (Showa 30), Typhoon No. 22 caused damage such as toppling an [[Enoki]] tree in the precincts{{Cite web|title=Kushida Shrine - Fallen Enoki Tree - Nishinippon Photo Library|url=http://c.nishinippon.co.jp/photolibrary/cat672/post_1691.php|website=c.nishinippon.co.jp|accessdate=2020-04-08|language=ja}}. == Gallery == File:Fukuoka Kushida kazariyama.jpg|Decorated Yamakasa float in the precincts File:Fukuoka kushida ginnan.jpg|The 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree, Kushida no Ginnan File:20100720 Fukuoka Kushida 3614.jpg|Celestial direction chart on the ceiling of the The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kushida-jinja

main hall | location = 1-41 Kamikawabatamachi, Hakata-ku, [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] | ISO = JP-40 | type degrees = 33 | type minutes = 35 | type seconds = 34.641 | longitude degrees = 130 | longitude minutes = 24 | longitude seconds = 37.814 | Label Position = left | festival Deities = [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami|Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami)

[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami) | Shrine rank = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]], [[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) | festival = [[May 3]]–[[May 4]] (Hakata Dontaku)
[[July 1]]–[[July 15]] (Hakata Gion Yamakasa) | map = Fukuoka city }} {{Nihongo|'''Kushida-jinja'''|櫛田神社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]], [[Japan]]. Dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo]], it is said to have been founded in 757. The [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] [[Matsuri|festival]] is centred on the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/showcase/english/theme/db_object_37.html |title=Kushida Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=24 March 2011}} ==Overview== '''Kushida Shrine''' (くしだじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Kamikawabatamachi, [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. It hosts festivals such as the [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] in July and the [[Hakata Okunchi]] in October. Although the [[Hakata Matsubayashi]] (Hakata Dontaku) in May is not strictly a Kushida Shrine festival, the Matsubayashi procession traditionally starts from Kushida Shrine{{Cite book|author=Kiyoshi Tanaka, Osamu Matsuda, Noriko Obayashi, Takashi Tomokiyo |title=Hakata Tabijō: Shiki no Gyoji Rekishi Yomoyama |publisher=Eizo Yagi |date=1967-04-25 |page=88}}{{Cite book|author=Seizo Inoue |title=Hakata Kyōdo Shi Jiten |publisher=Sanda Hisamoto |date=1987-11-01 |pages=264-266}}. == festival Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities: [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami), [[Amaterasu Ōmikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami). The main hall enshrines Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the left hall enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami, and the right hall enshrines Susanoo no Ōkami. Unlike other Kushida Shrines across Japan that mainly enshrine [[Kushinadahime]], Kushinadahime is not enshrined here, although there is a theory that this shrine originally enshrined her. Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami (Kushida no Ōkami) is believed to be a deity transferred from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]], [[Ise Province]]. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. There is a shrine dedicated to Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami in [[Sado City]], and among the five shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime, three are located in the Hokuriku region. == History == According to shrine tradition, Kushida Shrine was established in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) when the deity from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]] was transferred here, along with Amaterasu Ōmikami, who served the clan of Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the deity of the Matsusaka shrine. In [[Tengyō]] 4 (941), Ono no Yoshifuru prayed at [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto to suppress the [[Rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] and subsequently transferred Susanoo no Ōkami to this shrine after pacifying the rebellion. However, the most widely accepted theory is that in the late Heian period, [[Taira no Kiyomori]] transferred [[Kushida Shrine (Saga)|Kushida Shrine]] in [[Kanzaki]], [[Hizen Province]] (now Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture) to Hakata, a base for Japan-Song trade. This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. Karatsu City. Archived from the original on April 19, 2021. associated with [[Hata no Chikahira|Hata no Mikawa no Kami Chikahira]] of the [[Matsuura Clan]], whose peonies decorate the Yamakasa, was transplanted from Hizen Town, Karatsu City, in [[2018]] (Heisei 30){{Cite web|title=Hakata Gion Yamakasa and Peony Connection - Kushida Shrine in Fukuoka Receives Peony from Karatsu|url=https://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/308161|website=Saga Newspaper LiVE|accessdate=2019-12-02|language=ja}}. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kushida-jinja

[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami) | Shrine rank = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]], [[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) | festival = [[May 3]]–[[May 4]] (Hakata Dontaku)
[[July 1]]–[[July 15]] (Hakata Gion Yamakasa) | map = Fukuoka city }} {{Nihongo|'''Kushida-jinja'''|櫛田神社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]], [[Japan]]. Dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo]], it is said to have been founded in 757. The [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] [[Matsuri|festival]] is centred on the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/showcase/english/theme/db_object_37.html |title=Kushida Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=24 March 2011}} ==Overview== '''Kushida Shrine''' (くしだじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Kamikawabatamachi, [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. It hosts festivals such as the [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] in July and the [[Hakata Okunchi]] in October. Although the [[Hakata Matsubayashi]] (Hakata Dontaku) in May is not strictly a Kushida Shrine festival, the Matsubayashi procession traditionally starts from Kushida Shrine{{Cite book|author=Kiyoshi Tanaka, Osamu Matsuda, Noriko Obayashi, Takashi Tomokiyo |title=Hakata Tabijō: Shiki no Gyoji Rekishi Yomoyama |publisher=Eizo Yagi |date=1967-04-25 |page=88}}{{Cite book|author=Seizo Inoue |title=Hakata Kyōdo Shi Jiten |publisher=Sanda Hisamoto |date=1987-11-01 |pages=264-266}}. == festival Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities: [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami), [[Amaterasu Ōmikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami). The main hall enshrines Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the left hall enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami, and the right hall enshrines Susanoo no Ōkami. Unlike other Kushida Shrines across Japan that mainly enshrine [[Kushinadahime]], Kushinadahime is not enshrined here, although there is a theory that this shrine originally enshrined her. Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami (Kushida no Ōkami) is believed to be a deity transferred from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]], [[Ise Province]]. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. There is a shrine dedicated to Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami in [[Sado City]], and among the five shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime, three are located in the Hokuriku region. == History == According to shrine tradition, Kushida Shrine was established in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) when the deity from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]] was transferred here, along with Amaterasu Ōmikami, who served the clan of Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the deity of the Matsusaka shrine. In [[Tengyō]] 4 (941), Ono no Yoshifuru prayed at [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto to suppress the [[Rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] and subsequently transferred Susanoo no Ōkami to this shrine after pacifying the rebellion. However, the most widely accepted theory is that in the late Heian period, [[Taira no Kiyomori]] transferred [[Kushida Shrine (Saga)|Kushida Shrine]] in [[Kanzaki]], [[Hizen Province]] (now Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture) to Hakata, a base for Japan-Song trade. This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. Karatsu City. Archived from the original on April 19, 2021. associated with [[Hata no Chikahira|Hata no Mikawa no Kami Chikahira]] of the [[Matsuura Clan]], whose peonies decorate the Yamakasa, was transplanted from Hizen Town, Karatsu City, in [[2018]] (Heisei 30){{Cite web|title=Hakata Gion Yamakasa and Peony Connection - Kushida Shrine in Fukuoka Receives Peony from Karatsu|url=https://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/308161|website=Saga Newspaper LiVE|accessdate=2019-12-02|language=ja}}. In [[1955]] (Showa 30), Typhoon No. 22 caused damage such as toppling an [[Enoki]] tree in the precincts{{Cite web|title=Kushida Shrine - Fallen Enoki Tree - Nishinippon Photo Library|url=http://c.nishinippon.co.jp/photolibrary/cat672/post_1691.php|website=c.nishinippon.co.jp|accessdate=2020-04-08|language=ja}}. == Gallery == File:Fukuoka Kushida kazariyama.jpg|Decorated Yamakasa float in the precincts File:Fukuoka kushida ginnan.jpg|The 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree, Kushida no Ginnan File:20100720 Fukuoka Kushida 3614.jpg|Celestial direction chart on the ceiling of the tower gate File:Fukuoka Kushida Setsubun 2011.jpg|Giant Otafuku mask displayed around Setsubun File:Kushida Shrine02.jpg|Ema (votive plaques) in the precincts File:Kushida Shrine04.jpg|Chōzuya (purification pavilion) in the precincts File:Kushida shrine in Hakata wharf.jpg|Hama-miya at Hakata Wharf == Transportation == * [[Fukuoka City Subway]] [[Fukuoka City Subway Nanakuma Line|Nanakuma Line]] [[Kushida Shrine Station]] - 1 minute walk (140m). * [[Fukuoka City Subway]] [[Fukuoka City Subway Kūkō Line|Kūkō Line]] [[Gion Station (Fukuoka)|Gion Station]] - 5 minutes walk (400m). * Fukuoka City Subway Kūkō Line and [[Fukuoka City Subway Hakozaki Line|Hakozaki Line]] [[Nakasu-Kawabata Station]] - 8 minutes walk (660m). * [[Nishitetsu Bus]] - Get off at "Canal City Hakata-mae" or "Canal East Building-mae" bus stop. * [[Fukuoka Expressway Ring Road]] [[Gofukumachi Interchange]] - 1.2km. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{Citation style|date=March This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. Karatsu City. Archived from the original on April 19, 2021. associated with [[Hata no Chikahira|Hata no Mikawa no Kami Chikahira]] of the [[Matsuura Clan]], whose peonies decorate the Yamakasa, was transplanted from Hizen Town, Karatsu City, in [[2018]] (Heisei 30){{Cite web|title=Hakata Gion Yamakasa and Peony Connection - Kushida Shrine in Fukuoka Receives Peony from Karatsu|url=https://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/308161|website=Saga Newspaper LiVE|accessdate=2019-12-02|language=ja}}. In [[1955]] (Showa 30), Typhoon No. 22 caused damage such as toppling an [[Enoki]] tree in the precincts{{Cite web|title=Kushida Shrine - Fallen Enoki Tree - Nishinippon Photo Library|url=http://c.nishinippon.co.jp/photolibrary/cat672/post_1691.php|website=c.nishinippon.co.jp|accessdate=2020-04-08|language=ja}}. == Gallery == File:Fukuoka Kushida kazariyama.jpg|Decorated [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami) | Shrine rank = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]], [[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) | festival = [[May 3]]–[[May 4]] (Hakata Dontaku)
[[July 1]]–[[July 15]] (Hakata Gion Yamakasa) | map = Fukuoka city }} {{Nihongo|'''Kushida-jinja'''|櫛田神社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]], [[Japan]]. Dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo]], it is said to have been founded in 757. The [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] [[Matsuri|festival]] is centred on the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/showcase/english/theme/db_object_37.html |title=Kushida Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=24 March 2011}} ==Overview== '''Kushida Shrine''' (くしだじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Kamikawabatamachi, [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. It hosts festivals such as the [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] in July and the [[Hakata Okunchi]] in October. Although the [[Hakata Matsubayashi]] (Hakata Dontaku) in May is not strictly a Kushida Shrine festival, the Matsubayashi procession traditionally starts from Kushida Shrine{{Cite book|author=Kiyoshi Tanaka, Osamu Matsuda, Noriko Obayashi, Takashi Tomokiyo |title=Hakata Tabijō: Shiki no Gyoji Rekishi Yomoyama |publisher=Eizo Yagi |date=1967-04-25 |page=88}}{{Cite book|author=Seizo Inoue |title=Hakata Kyōdo Shi Jiten |publisher=Sanda Hisamoto |date=1987-11-01 |pages=264-266}}. == festival Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities: [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami), [[Amaterasu Ōmikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami). The main hall enshrines Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the left hall enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami, and the right hall enshrines Susanoo no Ōkami. Unlike other Kushida Shrines across Japan that mainly enshrine [[Kushinadahime]], Kushinadahime is not enshrined here, although there is a theory that this shrine originally enshrined her. Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami (Kushida no Ōkami) is believed to be a deity transferred from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]], [[Ise Province]]. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. There is a shrine dedicated to Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami in [[Sado City]], and among the five shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime, three are located in the Hokuriku region. == History == According to shrine tradition, Kushida Shrine was established in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) when the deity from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]] was transferred here, along with Amaterasu Ōmikami, who served the clan of Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the deity of the Matsusaka shrine. In [[Tengyō]] 4 (941), Ono no Yoshifuru prayed at [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto to suppress the [[Rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] and subsequently transferred Susanoo no Ōkami to this shrine after pacifying the rebellion. However, the most widely accepted theory is that in the late Heian period, [[Taira no Kiyomori]] transferred [[Kushida Shrine (Saga)|Kushida Shrine]] in [[Kanzaki]], [[Hizen Province]] (now Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture) to Hakata, a base for Japan-Song trade. This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. Karatsu City. Archived from the original on April 19, 2021. associated with [[Hata no Chikahira|Hata no Mikawa no Kami Chikahira]] of the [[Matsuura Clan]], whose peonies decorate the Yamakasa, was transplanted from Hizen Town, Karatsu City, in [[2018]] (Heisei 30){{Cite web|title=Hakata Gion Yamakasa and Peony Connection - Kushida Shrine in Fukuoka Receives Peony from Karatsu|url=https://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/308161|website=Saga Newspaper LiVE|accessdate=2019-12-02|language=ja}}. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kushida-jinja
櫛田神社 | image = Kushidajinjafukuoka01.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|35|34.7|N|130|24|37.7|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo]] | founded_by = | established = 757 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-41, Kamikawabata-machi, [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]]
[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] 812-0026 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kushida Shrine | image = [[File:Kushidajinjafukuoka01.jpg|250px]]
main hall | location = 1-41 Kamikawabatamachi, Hakata-ku, [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] | ISO = JP-40 | type degrees = 33 | type minutes = 35 | type seconds = 34.641 | longitude degrees = 130 | longitude minutes = 24 | longitude seconds = 37.814 | Label Position = left | festival Deities = [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami|Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami)
[[Amaterasu Ōmikami]]
[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami) | Shrine rank = Former [[Prefectural Shrine]], [[Beppyo Shrine]] | established = [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) | festival = [[May 3]]–[[May 4]] (Hakata Dontaku)
[[July 1]]–[[July 15]] (Hakata Gion Yamakasa) | map = Fukuoka city }} {{Nihongo|'''Kushida-jinja'''|櫛田神社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Hakata-ku, Fukuoka]], [[Japan]]. Dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo]], it is said to have been founded in 757. The [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] [[Matsuri|festival]] is centred on the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/showcase/english/theme/db_object_37.html |title=Kushida Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=24 March 2011}} ==Overview== '''Kushida Shrine''' (くしだじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Kamikawabatamachi, [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. The former [[shrine rank]] was [[Prefectural Shrine]]. It is situated in the old town area of Hakata and is affectionately known by locals as "'''Okushida-san'''." Its formal name is '''Hakata Sōchinshu Kushida Shrine''' (はかたそうちんじゅくしだじんじゃ). == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as the [[Ujigami]] and general [[Chinju]] (guardian deity) of Hakata. It hosts festivals such as the [[Hakata Gion Yamakasa]] in July and the [[Hakata Okunchi]] in October. Although the [[Hakata Matsubayashi]] (Hakata Dontaku) in May is not strictly a Kushida Shrine festival, the Matsubayashi procession traditionally starts from Kushida Shrine{{Cite book|author=Kiyoshi Tanaka, Osamu Matsuda, Noriko Obayashi, Takashi Tomokiyo |title=Hakata Tabijō: Shiki no Gyoji Rekishi Yomoyama |publisher=Eizo Yagi |date=1967-04-25 |page=88}}{{Cite book|author=Seizo Inoue |title=Hakata Kyōdo Shi Jiten |publisher=Sanda Hisamoto |date=1987-11-01 |pages=264-266}}. == festival Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities: [[Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami]] (Kushida no Ōkami), [[Amaterasu Ōmikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Ōkami]] (Gion no Ōkami). The main hall enshrines Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the left hall enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami, and the right hall enshrines Susanoo no Ōkami. Unlike other Kushida Shrines across Japan that mainly enshrine [[Kushinadahime]], Kushinadahime is not enshrined here, although there is a theory that this shrine originally enshrined her. Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami (Kushida no Ōkami) is believed to be a deity transferred from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]], [[Ise Province]]. Also known as Ōwakako no Mikoto, he is a descendant of [[Amenominakanushi]] and is said to have vanquished monsters in the Hokuriku region. There is a shrine dedicated to Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami in [[Sado City]], and among the five shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime, three are located in the Hokuriku region. == History == According to shrine tradition, Kushida Shrine was established in [[Tenpyō-hōji]] 1 (757) when the deity from [[Kushida Shrine (Matsusaka)|Kushida Shrine in Matsusaka]] was transferred here, along with Amaterasu Ōmikami, who served the clan of Ōhata-nushi no Ōkami, the deity of the Matsusaka shrine. In [[Tengyō]] 4 (941), Ono no Yoshifuru prayed at [[Yasaka Shrine]] in Kyoto to suppress the [[Rebellion of Fujiwara no Sumitomo]] and subsequently transferred Susanoo no Ōkami to this shrine after pacifying the rebellion. However, the most widely accepted theory is that in the late Heian period, [[Taira no Kiyomori]] transferred [[Kushida Shrine (Saga)|Kushida Shrine]] in [[Kanzaki]], [[Hizen Province]] (now Kanzaki City, Saga Prefecture) to Hakata, a base for Japan-Song trade. This theory is supported by the 'Hakata Yamakasa Record' compiled by the shrine priests and submitted to the Ministry of Education in [[1965]] and 'The History of Fukuoka' published by Fukuoka City in [[1979]], both of which indicate the shrine is a branch of Kushida Shrine in Kanzaki, Saga Prefecture. The shrine fell into decline during the [[Sengoku period]],'The location of Kushida Shrine in Hakata seems to have been given to the Jesuits by [[Ōtomo Yoshishige]], who built a church and a priest's residence there. According to 'History of the Jesuits in Japan' (Vol. 10, Western Kyushu Part 2, pp. 27-31, published September 20, 1979, translated by Kiichi Matsuda and Taro Kawasaki, Chūōkōronsha), there was a dispute between the Jesuits and the people of Hakata regarding the storage of tools used for the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival.' but in [[1587]] (Tensho 15), [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] rebuilt the shrine during the reconstruction of Hakata. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree in [[1868]] (Meiji 1), the shrine was managed by [[Jingū-ji]] belonging to [[Tōchō-ji]] during the [[Edo period]]. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), it was listed as a [[Prefectural Shrine]]. == Miscellaneous == The name of [[Gionmachi]] in [[Hakata-ku]], [[Fukuoka City]], is said to derive from Gion no Ōkami enshrined at this shrine[http://www.asocie.jp/reizen/profile/record/oldtown1.html History of Reizen District 1945-2007, Reizen District Union]. A decorated Yamakasa float is permanently displayed in the precincts. Since [[1960]] (Showa 35), [[Kyushu Asahi Broadcasting]] (KBC) has been airing 'Hashire! Yamakasa' as an offering. In addition, near the entrance of the precincts stands a 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree called "Kushida no Ginnan," which is mentioned in the '[[Hakata Iwaiuta]]' and is designated a natural monument of the prefecture. The "Cut-Tree Peony,"'[https://web.archive.org/web/20210419114128/https://www.city.karatsu.lg.jp/manabee/kyoiku/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazaihogo/kirigonobotan.html Prefectural Natural Monument Cut-Tree Peony]'. Karatsu City. Archived from the original on April 19, 2021. associated with [[Hata no Chikahira|Hata no Mikawa no Kami Chikahira]] of the [[Matsuura Clan]], whose peonies decorate the Yamakasa, was transplanted from Hizen Town, Karatsu City, in [[2018]] (Heisei 30){{Cite web|title=Hakata Gion Yamakasa and Peony Connection - Kushida Shrine in Fukuoka Receives Peony from Karatsu|url=https://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/308161|website=Saga Newspaper LiVE|accessdate=2019-12-02|language=ja}}. In [[1955]] (Showa 30), Typhoon No. 22 caused damage such as toppling an [[Enoki]] tree in the precincts{{Cite web|title=Kushida Shrine - Fallen Enoki Tree - Nishinippon Photo Library|url=http://c.nishinippon.co.jp/photolibrary/cat672/post_1691.php|website=c.nishinippon.co.jp|accessdate=2020-04-08|language=ja}}. == Gallery == File:Fukuoka Kushida kazariyama.jpg|Decorated Yamakasa float in the precincts File:Fukuoka kushida ginnan.jpg|The 1,000-year-old ginkgo tree, Kushida no Ginnan File:20100720 Fukuoka Kushida 3614.jpg|Celestial direction chart on the ceiling of the tower gate File:Fukuoka Kushida Setsubun 2011.jpg|Giant Otafuku mask displayed around Setsubun File:Kushida Shrine02.jpg|Ema (votive plaques) in the precincts File:Kushida Shrine04.jpg|Chōzuya (purification pavilion) in the precincts File:Kushida shrine in Hakata wharf.jpg|Hama-miya at Hakata Wharf == Transportation == * [[Fukuoka City Subway]] [[Fukuoka City Subway Nanakuma Line|Nanakuma Line]] [[Kushida Shrine Station]] - 1 minute walk (140m). * [[Fukuoka City Subway]] [[Fukuoka City Subway Kūkō Line|Kūkō Line]] [[Gion Station (Fukuoka)|Gion Station]] - 5 minutes walk (400m). * Fukuoka City Subway Kūkō Line and [[Fukuoka City Subway Hakozaki Line|Hakozaki Line]] [[Nakasu-Kawabata Station]] - 8 minutes walk (660m). * [[Nishitetsu Bus]] - Get off at "Canal City Hakata-mae" or "Canal East Building-mae" bus stop. * [[Fukuoka Expressway Ring Road]] [[Gofukumachi Interchange]] - 1.2km. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Books == {{Citation style|date=March 2018|section=1}} * Sukenari Azusa, Yoshihiko Umeda, editors and supervisors, ''Shinto Dictionary,'' Jinja Shinpōsha, [[1968]], p. 26. * Eiji Shirai, Masanori Toki, editors, ''Shrine Dictionary,'' [[Tōkyōdō Shuppan]], [[1979]], pp. 123-124. == External Links == {{commonscat|Kushida-jinja, Fukuoka}} * [https://www.hakatayamakasa.com/61866.html Kushida Shrine] - Hakata Gion Yamakasa official
